title: On Being a Modern Scientist and Being a Dietitian with a Blog
text:
On Being a Modern Scientist and Being a Dietitian with a Blog
In my statistics course this semester, we were instructed to read Jeff Leek’s book, How to be a Modern Scientist. The book is full of helpful tips for having a successful academic career and even ten years on from publishing remains relevant to my area of science.
One piece of academic advice that Dr. Leek give is to consider blogging. He’s clearly not the only person with this advice, as I am writing this blog post for an assignment to help start my academic professional footprint. But when I first read this advice, I was confused. Dietitians, including ones with PhDs, blog, but not in a way that is taken seriously by academia.
Having a “nutrition blog” has a very non-academic connotation, not something that I think aligns with what Dr. Leek was going for. A quick Google search for dietitian blogs yields thousands of RDs touting healthy recipes, tips for weight loss, and advertisements for private practice counseling sessions. I could not think of any colleague or mentor I’ve had in an academic space that has an academic-focused blog, but I can count a few who have tried to build a following through posting about diet plans while holding up a bowl of vibrant salad in a well-lit kitchen. Even when adding “PhD” or other academic-focused terms to a search, the blogs that come up feel like they are softening their intent to be more digestible for public consumption. One blogger I found, who posted an excellent review of an article on phosphorus binders, described their blog as “stories about what eating and meal planning means to me.”
Social media has also exploded with dietitians, in many ways replacing the traditional blog. Creators like Dr. Jessica Knurick, PhD, RD (@drjessicaknurick) and Dr. Rachel Paul, PhD, RD (@collegenutritionist) are some of the most-followed dietitians on Instagram and regularly include citations for articles, but similar to other dietitian-scientists, focus their work on public-focused content.
I think there are a multitude of reasons why blogging is more public-focused and less academic-focused in nutrition. There’s public demand for this type of content, financial incentives, the rampant battle against nutrition misinformation, the gender divide in nutrition and the connotations of being in a female-majority profession and having a blog. For many other areas of STEM, these aren’t factors.
As someone who hopes to be a clinician scientist someday, I like how my field leans into being clinicians and values using blogs as a communication tool to the general public. I wonder if there will come a day when academia can find more value in this type of work to the extent it could raise someone’s academic public profile in the way Dr. Leek hopes these platforms do.

title: Projects
text:
arXiv Preprint | Code
Over the centuries, I have devoted countless hours to deciphering and translating the ancient scripts and dialects of the various peoples of Middle-earth. My goal is to gain a deeper understanding of the cultures and histories of these peoples by studying their languages. Currently, I am working on a monograph that explores the linguistic roots of the Elvish languages. Through extensive research and analysis, I hope to shed light on the connections between the different dialects of Elvish and their origins. This project has been particularly challenging, as Elvish is a complex and nuanced language, but I am determined to see it through to completion.

title: Projects
section: The History of the War of the Ring
text:
The History of the War of the Ring

arXiv Preprint | Code
I am creating a comprehensive and detailed history of the conflict that goes beyond the surface-level events. By gathering information from a variety of sources, including my own memories, written accounts, and oral histories, I hope to shed new light on this important period in Middle-earth’s history and provide valuable insights into the motivations and actions of the various players involved.

title: You can’t live a full life on an empty stomach.
text:
LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  




You can’t live a full life on an empty stomach.
Welcome! I am Allison Thirion Blasius, MPH, RD, LD, FAND, a registered dietitian and doctoral student.
I have been a Registered Dietitian since 2018 and have practiced in a variety of pediatric settings, including eating disorders, neurology, surgery, pulmonolgy, general outpatient counseling, school nutrition. Currently, I practice at UH Rainbow Babies and Children’s Hospital and sub-specailize in both pediatric endocrinology and pediatric kidney disease. I also work with Camp Ho Mita Koda, the nation’s oldest camp for children with type 1 diabetes.
My research interests center around nutritional quality of life in children, especially those with chronic conditions like type 1 diabetes and end-stage renal disease. I am also interested in the impact that public health nutrition programs like the National School Lunch Program (NSLP) have on childhood health outcomes and how policies dictating these programs can be optimized to promote the long-term health of all children. Currently, I work under the mentorship of Dr. Melissa Prescott on a variety of projects regarding diet quality, nutrition education, and NLSP policy.
While I enjoy research, education is my passion. I have been an adjunct lecturer at Case Western Reserve University since 2023, where I teach introductory nutrition courses at the undergraduate level. I also support the UH Dietetic Internship as part of my clinical role and have precepted over 75 students since becoming an RD.
Almost everything you need to know about me professionally can be found on my CV.
Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions or would like to discuss potential projects.
